coredata.dc:title: A luminescence nanosensor for Ornidazole detection using graphene quantum dots entrapped in silica molecular imprinted polymer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mehrzad-Samarin, Mina
Faridbod, Farnoush
Ganjali, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: A luminescence nanosensor has been developed for analysis of Ornidazole in biological samples using graphene-quantum-dot-embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer (GQD-SMIP) as a selective probe for this analyte. The GQD-SMIP was found to possess a strong fluorescent emission at 450 nm upon excitation at 365 nm. This emission was found to linearly quench in the presence of Ornidazole in a concentration range of 0.75 to 30 μM. A detection limit of 0.24 μM was reached using the probe and the sensor was successfully used in the determination of the analyte in plasma samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanosensor
Luminescent detection
Graphene quantum dots
Ornidazole
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Silica

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of GQDs-embedded SMIP, (b) FT-IR spectra of silica-GQDs.
(a) TEM image of GQDs-embedded SMIP; (b) SEM image of GQDs -embedded SMIP; (c) SEM image of GQD; (d) SEM image of GQDs-embedded SNIP.
(a) UV-VIS spectra of GQDs-embedded SMIP, (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-embedded SMIP.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-embedded SNIP, GQDs-embedded SMIP before and after template molecules extraction, GQDs-embedded SMIP with addition of 1 mM of ONZ. The concentration of GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs-embedded SNIP was 2 mg mL−1.
(b) Fluorescence emission of GQDs-embedded SMIP before and after template removal.

Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs-embedded SNIP with addition of various amount of 50 μM of ONZ in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 9) and related Stern-Volmer plots from GQDs-embedded SMIP and SNIP with ONZ. The concentration of GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs -embedded SNIP was 2.5 mg mL−1.

(a) Selective adsorption of ONZ, MNZ, DMZ and CAM (2 μM) by GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs-embedded SNIP at pH = 9 (PBS buffer, 10 mM), the concentration of GQDs-embedded SMIP and GQDs-embedded SNIP was 2.5 mg mL−1; (b) Influence of coexisting common ions (10 μM) on the fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of GQDs-embedded SMIP in the presence of ONZ (5 μM).
Schematic of the preparation of GQDs-embedded SMIP.
Results of the analysis of plasma samples.

head.ce:title: A luminescence nanosensor for Ornidazole detection using graphene quantum dots entrapped in silica molecular imprinted polymer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Alborz Campus, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biosensor Research Center, Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A luminescence nanosensor has been developed for analysis of Ornidazole in biological samples using graphene-quantum-dot-embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer (GQD-SMIP) as a selective probe for this analyte. The GQD-SMIP was found to possess a strong fluorescent emission at 450 nm upon excitation at 365 nm. This emission was found to linearly quench in the presence of Ornidazole in a concentration range of 0.75 to 30 μM. A detection limit of 0.24 μM was reached using the probe and the sensor was successfully used in the determination of the analyte in plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The application of nanomaterials constitutes an important area in designing optical sensors/biosensors [1–4], and consequently the application of fluorescent nanomaterials is candidate for use in the construction of optical sensing systems [5–9]. Quantum dots (QDs), for instance, are among the fluorescent nanomaterials used in this regard [10–13], due to their very high optical quantum yields, narrow and symmetrical spectra, broad excitation spectra and photo-stability [14,15]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an important class of quantum dots offering advantages like low toxicity, strong fluorescence, inertness, and good stability under light [16].

Various approaches have been used for designing QD-based sensors [17,18]. A rather general approach applied for enhancing the selectivity behavior QD-based nanosensors is together with molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs), which are biomimetic polymers commonly synthesized through polymerizing so-called functional and cross-linking monomers in the presence of a template molecule [19]. The template should be able to form complexes with the monomers. Once the reaction is over and the polymer is formed the template is removed. Yet the MIPs include cavities specifically fitting the shape and chemical functionality of the template, and can recognize the template through an enzyme-substrate-like mechanism [20].

Entrapping GQDs in fluorescent MIPs can lead to enhancements like the high sensitivity of emission due to the high selectivity of the MIP. Once a target molecule is the analyte and enters the cavities of fluorescent MIP, the fluorescence behavior of the quantum dots alters [11,21], which can be used as a means for the determination of the analyte. However, reviewing the literature indicates fewer reports on this novel nano-technology-based analytical strategy [22,23].

Both organic and inorganic polymers can be imprinted. The sol–gel technique is the most conventional approach used in the case of inorganic polymers. This technique enjoys advantages such as eco-friendly solvents like water or ethanol [24]. Various QDs/MIP-based fluorescent nanosensors have recently been designed for analytes, like the application of GQDs for metronidazole [19], Mn-doped ZnS QDs for cyphenothrin [21], carbon QDs for tetracycline [22], and GQDs and carbon dot embedded MIP for paranitrophenol [23].

In this work, as continue of our previous studies, a selective and sensitive luminescence nano-sensor was developed using a GQD-embedded silica MIP (SMIP) and successfully applied for the determination of Ornidazole (ONZ) in plasma samples.

ONZ is an antiprotozoal/antibacterial compound used against anaerobic bacteria. The compound is preferred for use in treating conditions like severe hepatic and intestinal amoebiasis, giardiasis, and trichomoniasis of the urogenital tract, bacterial vaginosis, prophylaxis of susceptible anaerobic infections in dental and gastric surgery; and helicobacter pylori duodenal ulcers [25]. In this light, the determination of ONZ has great importance.

Analytical grade reagents were used as without any further treatments. Citric acid, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS), methanol, ethanol and other chemical reagents were obtained from Merck Chem. Co (Germany). A PBS buffer (pH = 9) was also. Ornidazole (ONZ) and other standards were obtained from a domestic Iranian company. Double distilled water (DDW) was used through the experiments.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy experiments were performed using a Perkin-Elmer instrument and the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on a Philips CM30 TEM. A Hitachi S4160 electron microscope was used to perform the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) studies. Finally fluorescence measurements were performed using a Perkin-Elmer LS50 luminescence spectrometer.

To prepare GQDs, citric acid was pyrolyzed according to a previously reported method [26,27]. Typically 2 g of the compound was transferred in to a 25-mL flask, where it was heated to 200 °C for half an hour when it changed to a light orange liquid. Fig. 1 shows TEM image of the prepared GQDs.

The general procedure for the preparation of GQD-SMIP involved adding 10 mmol of APTS to the solution of the prepared GQD. The resulting solution was kept under stirring for half an hour at 40 °C, and then allowed to cool to ambient temperature. The product, i.e. the APTS-modified GQDs (GQD-APTS) was kept for further experiments. The SMIPs were prepared through the embedding GQDs in silica matrix via a typical sol-gel polymerization. In a 25 mL-beaker, 10 mg ONZ (as a template molecule) was added to 5 mL APTS-modified GQDs under stirring [28]. This was followed by adding 3 mL of APTS and 2 mL of TEOS to the reaction mixture, respectively. Then, 0.5 mL of a catalytic solution prepared by mixing 2 g of NH4F, 100 mL of H2O and 23 mL NH4OH, was added to the reaction beaker. The final mixture was next stirred for an extra 30 min. The original ONZ template molecules were removed using a methanol, and this was continued until UV analysis did not show any ONZ in the washing solvent. The pure composites were dried at 60 °C overnight to eliminate the solvent completely. Eventually the GQDs-SMIP sample was dispersed in 40 mL of water and stored at 4 °C for later use. Non-imprinted polymer (NIP) was also embedded with GQDs (GQDs-SNIP) prepared under similar conditions except for the absence of ONZ (the template molecule), and used as a control sample.

The ability of the GQDs-SMIP based sensor was evaluated using solutions with different concentrations of ONZ through fluorescence spectrometry at 450 using an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The slit width was 15 nm for both excitation and emission. The analyses were performed in a 4.5-mL quartz cell with a 10 mm width.

The stock solution of GQD-SMIP was diluted using a PBS (pH = 9) and next 100 μL of the above mixture and various quantities of a standard 20 μM ONZ solution were injected to the cuvette in sequence and the solutions were shaken vigorously. Further, control experiments were performed using GQD-SNIP under identical conditions.

Drug-free human plasma was received from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Organization (Tehran, Iran). The samples were kept at −20 °C. The plasma proteins were precipitated, by treating the samples with 20 μL of a 1 mol L−1nitric acid solution. Then the mixture was vortexed for a 30s and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. Final samples were prepared by taking 100 μl aliquots of the supernatant and diluting them 100 times.

GQDs which are prepared through a bottom-up strategy by pyrolysis of citric acid, in fact, are graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) which due to the inappropriate but simple terminology called GQDs. In such structures, some functionalized groups such as carboxylic acids exist on the edge of the sheets which improve their modifications and water-dispersion abilities. In case of GQD-SMIP polymerization reaction, APTS acted as functional monomer, and TEOS as cross-linker. The interaction of carboxylic groups of GQDs with amine group of APTES and formation of NH3 + and COO−. Previously studies shows that at least two layer of the APTES were coated on GOQDs [28] and some amine groups from APTES are free to interact with functional groups of the template molecule ONZ. To enhance the interactions among the GQDs and the silica matrix, GQDs was embedded in silica prior to the sol–gel hydrolysis and the condensation imprinting polymerization reaction [29]. After removal of the template molecule, the GQD-SMIP was characterized. Scheme 1 shows schematic of the preparation of GQDs -embedded SMIP.

Fig. 2(a) illustrates a typical FT-IR spectrum of GQD-SMIP. The wide and intense signals around 1072 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric stretching of Si-O-C and Si-O-Si [30], indicating that some silica interacted with GQDs. The bands around 760 cm−1 and 460 cm−1 are also attributed to the SiO vibrations. Further the broad absorption peaks at 3440 cm−1 (corresponding to the NH bond) and 2900 cm−1 (due to the CH stretching) indicate the presence of aminopropyl groups. Also the signal at 1650 cm−1 indicates the presence of carbonyl (-CO-) group in the sample. As shown in the FT-IR absorption spectrum of GQD-SMIP, peaks of Si–O–Si (at 1120 cm−1) and Si–O–C (at 1072 cm−1) become stronger compared with those of silica-GQDs (SNIP) (Fig. 2(b)). The obtained results confirm the copolymerization of TEOS and APTS on the surface of the sample.

The results of TEM and SEM studies on the GQD-SMIP samples are presented in Fig. 3(a) and (b), which indicate the particles to have an average diameter of around 100 nm. The different morphologies of the GQD-SMIP and GQD-SNIP samples is also shown in Fig. 3(c) and (d).

The GQD-SMIP samples revealed a wide UV–Vis adsorption band extending between 300 and 400 nm, which was attributed to the aromatic π system, band gap, and absorption edge of the GQDs. Upon excitation at 365 nm, the GQD-SMIP emitted an asymmetric fluorescence emission signal at 450 nm (Fig. 4 ). The results indicate the silica coating as being optically transparent and inert and not limited the PL properties of GQDs [31].

The fluorescence of GQD-SMIP was used for sensing ONZ in sample solutions. Based on Fig. 5 (a,b), removing the template enhances the fluorescence intensity of the GQD-SMIP samples and an opposite trend was observed in the presence of ONZ molecules. This has been declared to be the result of energy transfer among GQDs and ONZ [32].

The influence of the pH of the test solution on the fluorescence ratio of GQD-SMIP probe ((F0/F-1); F0 and F being the fluorescence intensity of the probe before and after adding ONZ) was investigated in the range of 3.0 to 11.0. The observations indicated that the fluorescence quenching caused by ONZ increases by increasing pH from 3.0 to 9.0, and then shows a slight decrease from pH 9.0 to 11.0. Accordingly 9.0 was chosen as the best pH and the pH of all test solutions was set at this value.

The reason for this behavior could be that in acidic pH (high concentrations of H+) the heteroatoms present in ONZ or even the binding sites of GQD-SMIP are protonated hindering the interactions of the probe and the target molecule. Another reason is that the maximum absorbance of ONZ shifts to lower wave lengths [33] in acidic media, decreasing the chance of photo induced electron transfers between GQD and ONZ.
Upon the binding of ONZ to the sites of GQD-SMIP, the energy transfer from GQD-SMIP to ONZ lowers the excitement energy and the emission will hence decrease.

The possible mechanism of the fluorescence quenching observed in the present study can be a kind of photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [34]. Since there is no spectral overlap between GQDs and ONZ, the quenching phenomenon can be due to the electronic interaction between the fluorophores in the excited states as the donors and the ONZ as the acceptors. When a molecule is excited by a photon, one of the electrons in a ground state can be excited to a higher energy level, which leaves a vacancy in the ground state. This vacancy can hence be occupied by an electron from a donor, and the electron in the higher energy level can be donated to an acceptor. Hence, a photo-excited molecule can act as an oxidizing or a reducing agent depends on the donor atoms. In either case an electron is transferred to a higher energy orbital, which is referred to as a charge-separated electron-hole pair in the case of semiconductors. When no proper electron donor or acceptor species is available the molecules can normally emit fluorescence radiation.

The amine groups present on the surface of GQD-SMIP and SNIP can form hydrogen bonds with ONZ. The plot of the fluorescent intensity of GQD-SMIP, under equilibrium in a PBS (pH = 9) against the ONZ concentration from 0 to 40 μmol L−1 is given in Fig. 6 . Although the fluorescence emission of both GQD-SMIP and SNIP quenches in the presence of ONZ, this phenomenon is much more considerable in the case of GQD-SMIP. The non-imprinted composites GQD-SNIP did not show obvious difference with the addition of ONZ. This could be attributed to the suitable sites present in GQD-SMIP for interaction with ONZ molecules making it more sensitive towards ONZ concentration.

The fluorescence quenching efficiency is described by the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
Q
where F0 and F are the in the absence and at given concentrations of the quenching agent (here ONZ), Q is the concentration of the quenching agent, and Ksv is a constant which depends on the nature of the target molecule. Fig. 5 shows the Stern-Volmer plots for GQD-SMIP and GQD-SNIP in the presence of ONZ in concentrations ranging from 75 nM to 30 μM. The plots are linear in both cases and the regression equations were 0.996 and 0.966 for SMIP and SNIP, respectively.

By increasing the concentration of ONZ, the adsorption on GQD-SMIP and SNIP increased rapidly. The number of ONZ/GQD-SMIP bonds is however higher than that in the case of GQD-SNIP, which is attributed to the efficiency of the MIP used for selective and specific interactions.

The proposed sensor was then applied for the quantitative analysis of trace ONZ. The calibration obtained using the sensor was obtained in the range of 0.75 to 30 μmol L−1, and according to Fig. 5, the detection limit (3σ) [35] reached as low as 0.245 μmol L−1.

The repeatability and reproducibility of the results were also satisfactory (RSD 4.6% and 5.5%, respectively). The reproducibility of the results was examined through evaluating the precisions of the results of determining ONZ concentrations three times for three days and the RSD was 4.4%.

The GQD-SMIP nanosensor was used for the determination of ONZ concentration in plasma samples. The recovery values were evaluated by comparing the average results observed for spiked plasma samples and those of standard solutions with the same concentration (for four replicate measurements). As indicated by the results (Table 1 ), the proposed method offers a wide dynamic range, as well as good accuracy and low detection limit and it can hence be an appropriate tool for the fast on-site analysis of ONZ in samples with complex matrices.

The selectivity behavior of the sensor was evaluated using the compounds with chemical structures analogous to the ONZ (i.e. Metronidazole (MNZ), Dimetridazole (DMZ) and chloramphenicol (CAM)) (Fig. 6(a)). The results clearly show that the sensor is very selective to the analyte (template), which can be attributed to the fact that in the course of the synthesis process, various specific recognition sites are arranged with respect to the template in MIPs, and the sites are complementary to the template in terms of shape, size, and spatial arrangement. Consequently, ONZ can strongly bind to the imprinted sites and cause considerable changes in the fluorescence behavior of the GQDs. On the other hand, with the NIPs fluorescence quenching of the analogs and ONZ were quite close but very slight, indicating that NIPs cannot effectively bind to any of the compounds tested (Fig. 7(b)).

To evaluate the effects of foreign co-existing substances on the analysis of ONZ, further experiments were conducted using ONZ samples containing other ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, NO3 −, Cl−), and as shown in Fig. 7(b) no obvious effect could be observed, further supporting the selectivity and specificity of the interaction mechanism of the sensor and ONZ.

The through combining fluorescence detection and imprinting technique a selective and sensitive sensor for the direct determination of ONZ was developed. The prepared GQD-SMIP composites were used for the detection of trace amounts of ONZ in plasma samples and the results were found to be satisfactory. This optical sensor has a low detection limit and offers good accuracy in the case of samples with complex matrices, which reveals its great potentials for it use in advanced analytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Research Council of University of Tehran for the financial support of this work.
